content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 26, 2002

Hilda Cantú Montoy, City Attorney
City of Fresno
2600 Fresno Street
Fresno, CA 93721-3602

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-02-037

Dear Ms. Montoy:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that this letter should not be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. This advice is applicable and confers immunity only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct, and that all of the material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71; Govt. Code § 83114.)

QUESTIONS

1. May Mayor Autry participate in the decision to have the City of Fresno designated as the FLICS designee?

2. Would the Mayor have a conflict of interest in the event the City of Fresno becomes a FLICS Designee, and the Mayor participates in any of the decisionmaking and operations of FLICS?

_________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2; sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-02-037
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1 and 2. Mayor Autry may participate in decisions regarding the city's FLICS
designation if it is not reasonably foreseeable that his economic interests will be
materially financially affected as discussed below.

FACTS

The California Film Commission ("CFC") was created under Government Code
Section 14998 et seq. to facilitate, retain, and attract filming in California. The
partnership between CFC and local in-state film offices or commissions, referred to as
Film Liaisons in California Statewide ("FLICS"), is an integral part of the CFC's effort to
improve the filming environment in California. Any city or county public
governmental agency ("public entity") located in California with jurisdiction over filming
in its geographic area may apply for CFC's endorsement as a participant in the FLICS
partnership and assign an office, organization, or nonprofit to be a "FLICS Designee."
The role of an approved FLICS Designee is to help carry out the goals and objectives of
promoting film in its area in collaboration with CFC and other FLICS Designees.

According to the CFC, no two FLICS designees may represent the same public
entity or the same geographic filming jurisdiction. In the case of overlap, where a public
entity submits an application for FLICS Designee already covered by an existing FLICS
Designee, the CFC will defer to the existing FLICS Designee for resolution.

The Fresno Convention and Visitor's Bureau ("Bureau"), a 501(c) nonprofit
corporation, is currently the FLICS Designee for the City and County of Fresno. The
Mayor of the City of Fresno sits on the board of the Bureau, which consists of 23
members, including other city and county elected officials. The Bureau is funded by
independent agreements with the City of Fresno and the County of Fresno, and by
membership dues. The purpose of the Bureau is to promote the City and County of
Fresno as a site for meetings, conventions, and trade shows, and as a destination for
pleasure travel by soliciting and servicing such business and encouraging hospitality for
the City and County's visitors.

The City wants to obtain its own local designation as a FLICS Designee. The city
may become a FLICS Designee if CFC receives written notice that the public entities that
endorsed the FLICS Designee adopted a resolution rescinding the resolution designating
the existing FLICS Designee, or the FLICS Designee submits a written request to have its
FLICS designation removed. A FLICS Designee may also lose its designation if it
receives written notice from CFC that it violated one of the requirements in the FLICS
Code of Responsibilities or is no longer eligible. City staff are currently working on
obtaining the city's designation as a FLICS Designee, either by agreement by the public
entities that endorsed the Bureau as the FLICS Designee, or by having the Bureau request
that its local designation be removed.

Alan Autry is the mayor of the City of Fresno. The mayor is a professional actor
and has economic interests relating to the film industry. Those interests include, but are

File No. A-02-037
Page No. 3

not limited to, Dirt Road Productions, a film production company located in Clovis,
California (neighboring the City of Fresno and in the County of Fresno). His economic
interests relating to the film industry are disclosed in his Statement of Economic Interests.
Neither the mayor nor his production company will use any of the services provided by
CFC or FLICS. The mayor will also not accept any acting positions with any film
production generated by the CFC or FLICS, except for charitable purposes.

ANALYSIS

The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. (§ 87100.) Pursuant to regulation 18700, an eight-step
analysis is applied to determine whether a public official has a conflict of interest in a
given governmental decision.

Steps One and Two: Is the individual a "public official," and if so, is the public
official making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?

Your letter correctly assumes that as the mayor of Fresno he will be making,
participating in making, or influencing decisions in regard to the FLICS designation as a
public official of a local government agency. (§§ 82041, 82048; Reg. 18702.1.)

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702.1.) A
public official "participates in making a governmental decision" when, acting within the
authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or intervening review,
the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker
regarding the governmental decision. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702.2.) A public
official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a decision before his
or her own agency if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears
before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Section
87100; Regulation 18702.3.)

Thus, the mayor may not make, participate in or influence any decision which
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his economic interests.

File No. A-02-037
Page No. 4

Step Three: What is the "economic interest" of the public official?

The next step in the analysis is to determine what, if any, economic interest the
public official may have in the governmental decision at issue. There are six types of
economic interests that a public official may have. Also, a public official may have an
indirect economic interest through a member of his or her immediate family. (§ 87103.)

Business Interests. An official has an economic interest in a business entity in which the
official, the official's spouse, or the official's dependent children or anyone acting on the
official's behalf has invested $2,000 or more, or in which the official is a director, officer,
employee, or holds any position of management. (§ 87103, suds. (a), (d).)

Sources of Income. The official has an economic interest in anyone, whether an
individual or an organization, from whom the official has received (or by whom the
official has been promised) $500 or more in income within the 12 months prior to the
decision. (§ 87103, subd. (c).)

Your letter specifically refers to the mayor's interest in Dirt Road Productions, a
film production company located in the City of Clovis, neighboring the City of Fresno
and in the County of Fresno. According to Mr. Autry's financial disclosure statement,
Mr. Autry is President of the production company, is a greater than 10% owner, and
receives a salary from the company. Therefore, Mr. Autry has an economic interest in
Dirt Road Productions.

Step Four: Are the public official's economic interests directly or indirectly involved
in the decision?

Business Entity/Source of Income

A person, including a business entity or source of income, is directly involved in a
decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

(2) is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency.
A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person." (Reg. 18704.1, subd.(a).)

Under the Commission's regulations, business entities and sources of income
which are not directly involved in a decision under the rules stated above are considered
indirectly involved. (Reg. 18704.1, subd.(b).)

File No. A-02-037
Page No. 5

From the facts you provided, Mayor Autry's economic interests in Dirt Road
Productions are indirectly involved in the specific decision to designate Fresno as the
FLICS Designee. (Regs. 18704.1, 18704.2.) With respect to future decisions of the
council, however, we do not have sufficient information at this time to make such a
determination.

Steps Five and Six: Will the financial effect of the decision on the public official's
economic interests be material and reasonably foreseeable?

Whether the financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's
economic interest is material and reasonably foreseeable depends upon the facts and
circumstances of each particular case. An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if
there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required. However, if
the effect is a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198.) The Commission has developed materiality standards for the various
types of economic interests to determine whether there will be a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on such interests.

Business Entity/Source of Income

The financial effect of a governmental decision on a small business entity,² which
is not directly involved in the decision, is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a
fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or more; or,
(B) The governmental decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing
or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $5,000 or more; or,
(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or
liabilities of $20,000 or more." (Regulation 18705.1(c)(4).)

Consequently, in any decision that foreseeably will affect Mayor Autry's business
in the amounts described above, this materiality standard will be met and Mayor Autry
will have a "financial interest" in that decision. The same standard also applies in the
analysis of Mayor Autry's business as a source of income. Mayor Autry must assess
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that his business will be materially financially
affected according to the monetary thresholds identified above with respect to any
decision he makes, participates in making, or influences.³

___________________________
² Please refer to the enclosed copy of Regulation 18705.1 for the criteria applicable to businesses
of different sizes.

³ The facts of your letter do not implicate, and we do not analyze, steps Seven and Eight of the
analysis which relate to exceptions to the conflict of interest rules.

File No. A-02-037
Page No. 6

With respect to other financial interests in future decisions, the conflicts analysis
will follow the analysis outlined above. Because we cannot make necessarily factual
determinations on the foreseeable effects of unknown decisions, Mayor Autry must
assess whether it is reasonably foreseeable that his business will be materially financially
affected according to the monetary thresholds identified above. To do so, Mr. Autry
must determine whether his economic interests are directly or indirectly involved in
subsequent decisions as the details of each decision become known and disqualify
himself it is reasonably foreseeable that his business will be materially financially
affected. This includes decisions he makes, participates in making, or influences as the
mayor of Fresno involving the decisionmaking and operations of FLICS (if the City
becomes a FLICS Designee).⁴

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: C. Scott Pocher
Counsel, Legal Division

CST:jg
I:\AdviceLtrs\02-037.

Enclosure

_______________________

⁴ The facts of your letter do not implicate, and we do not analyze, steps Seven and Eight of the
analysis which relate to exceptions to the conflict of interest rules.
